Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd (under judicial management)

Facts.Text: The company operated as a ship chartering and management entity within a group of companies with interlocking business interests[cite: 4566, 4567].
The company engaged a specific law firm to provide advice on available restructuring options during a period of financial distress[cite: 4572, 4574].
The company was placed under interim judicial management by a court order, transferring management of its affairs, business, and property to independent officeholders[cite: 4584, 4701].

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company, acting through its directors after its management powers have been vested in judicial managers, retains the authority to initiate legal proceedings in its own name[cite: 4531, 4661].
Party_Details.Rule: Upon the appointment of judicial managers, all powers conferred on directors by the Companies Act or the company's constitution are exercised by the judicial managers to the exclusion of the directors[cite: 4713, 4732, 4866].
Party_Details.Application: The directors sought to cause the company to bring an injunction against its former solicitors after management authority had passed to the judicial managers. The Defendant argued that the directors were divested of such powers by the statutory insolvency regime[cite: 4542, 4550, 4833].
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the powers of the directors, including the power to appoint solicitors and to litigate in the company's name, were displaced and vested in the insolvency officeholders. While directors retain narrow residuary powers to challenge the validity of the insolvency appointment itself, these powers do not extend to commencing substantive operational litigation. Consequently, the directors lacked standing to cause the company to bring the action [cite: 4730, 4833, 4881-4883].

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP

Facts.Text: From the early 1990s, the firm advised the family and the group of companies they owned on various commercial matters[cite: 4599, 4600].
The firm was formally retained to advise the company on issues arising from its insolvency and to file restructuring applications[cite: 4572, 4579].
Following the appointment of insolvency officeholders, the firm was retained by those officeholders to continue providing legal services to the company[cite: 4540, 4584].

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a law firm validly retained by a company's judicial managers can be subject to an injunction initiated by directors who have been divested of their management authority[cite: 4531, 4618].
Party_Details.Rule: The power to appoint and instruct solicitors for a company under judicial management lies exclusively with the appointed officeholders[cite: 4724, 4734, 4879].
Party_Details.Application: The firm acted under the authorization of the judicial managers, who held the sole statutory power to represent the company. The directors challenged this retention without satisfying the procedural requirements for standing[cite: 4618, 4619, 4655].
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court did not reach the substantive issues of conflict because it found the injunction proceedings were not properly brought. Because the directors lacked the legal standing to cause the companies to sue the firm, the actions were struck out as unsustainable[cite: 4885, 4890].

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd (under judicial management)

Facts.Text: The company was a major oil trading entity that encountered severe financial difficulties in early 2020[cite: 4566, 4571].
The company engaged legal counsel to advise on its insolvency and to manage a proposed debt restructuring exercise[cite: 4572, 4579].
A High Court order placed the company under interim judicial management, divesting the directors of their powers to manage the entity's affairs[cite: 4584, 4701].

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company under judicial management can maintain a substantive legal action against its solicitors where that action is directed by directors acting without the consent of the judicial managers[cite: 4531, 4587, 4661].
Party_Details.Rule: A company under judicial management must act through its managers. Directors are effectively functus officio regarding management powers, including the conduct of litigation[cite: 4732, 4866, 4882].
Party_Details.Application: The directors unilaterally purported to act on behalf of the company to restrain the law firm retained by the judicial managers. The Defendant sought to strike out the action on the basis that the directors had no authority to use the company's name for this purpose[cite: 4589, 4655, 4656].
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that the power to bring or proceed with the injunction action was a management power exclusively vested in the interim judicial managers and judicial managers by the court orders and the Companies Act. The directors' residuary powers did not extend to such operational management decisions. The action was therefore struck out for lack of standing[cite: 4748, 4881, 4885].

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP

Facts.Text: The firm was retained to advise on restructuring and subsequently received confidential financial statements and management accounts belonging to the company[cite: 4572, 4602, 4603].
Following the company's entry into interim judicial management, the appointed managers continued the firm's retainer[cite: 4584, 4642].

Party_Details.Issue: Whether solicitors can continue to represent a company and its managers when their authority is challenged by the company's former directors[cite: 4531, 4631].
Party_Details.Rule: A company must act through its appointed insolvency officeholders; once appointed, these officeholders have the sole authority to manage the company's legal representation[cite: 4734, 4879].
Party_Details.Application: The firm acted under the express authorization of the only individuals with the legal authority to represent the company. The directors' attempt to 'restrain' the firm was a non-starter because they lacked the requisite authority to manage the company's litigation[cite: 4618, 4655, 4748].
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court struck out the action against the firm. It determined that because the power to litigate in the company's name had been transferred to the officeholders, the directors had no standing to bring the claim. The firm was not improperly acting, and the attempt to injunct them was legally unsustainable[cite: 4885, 4890].
